title: Vertical Order Traversal of a Binary Tree

testcases.input: root = [3,9,20,null,null,15,7] Output: [[9],[3,15],[20],[7]] Explanation: Column -1: Only node 9 is in this column. Column 0: Nodes 3 and 15 are in this column in that order from top to bottom. Column 1: Only node 20 is in this column. Column 2: Only node 7 is in this column.
testcases.expectedOutput: [[9],[3,15],[20],[7]] Explanation: Column -1: Only node 9 is in this column. Column 0: Nodes 3 and 15 are in this column in that order from top to bottom. Column 1: Only node 20 is in this column. Column 2: Only node 7 is in this column.

testcases.input: root = [1,2,3,4,5,6,7] Output: [[4],[2],[1,5,6],[3],[7]] Explanation: Column -2: Only node 4 is in this column. Column -1: Only node 2 is in this column. Column 0: Nodes 1, 5, and 6 are in this column. 1 is at the top, so it comes first. 5 and 6 are at the same position (2, 0), so we order them by their value, 5 before 6. Column 1: Only node 3 is in this column. Column 2: Only node 7 is in this column.
testcases.expectedOutput: [[4],[2],[1,5,6],[3],[7]] Explanation: Column -2: Only node 4 is in this column. Column -1: Only node 2 is in this column. Column 0: Nodes 1, 5, and 6 are in this column. 1 is at the top, so it comes first. 5 and 6 are at the same position (2, 0), so we order them by their value, 5 before 6. Column 1: Only node 3 is in this column. Column 2: Only node 7 is in this column.

testcases.input: root = [1,2,3,4,6,5,7] Output: [[4],[2],[1,5,6],[3],[7]] Explanation: This case is the exact same as example 2, but with nodes 5 and 6 swapped. Note that the solution remains the same since 5 and 6 are in the same location and should be ordered by their values.
testcases.expectedOutput: [[4],[2],[1,5,6],[3],[7]] Explanation: This case is the exact same as example 2, but with nodes 5 and 6 swapped. Note that the solution remains the same since 5 and 6 are in the same location and should be ordered by their values.
